context.text: The AI RMF core is organized around Govern, Map, Measure, and Manage. Map frames the system's intended use, context, stakeholders, benefits, and risks. Measure analyzes, assesses, benchmarks, and monitors risks and related impacts. Manage prioritizes and acts on mapped and measured risks.
context.title: NIST AI RMF 1.0 - Core Functions

context.text: The Playbook gives suggested actions and prompts for teams applying the Govern, Map, Measure, and Manage functions to an AI system. It is a companion implementation aid, not a separate risk taxonomy.
context.title: NIST AI RMF Playbook Overview

context.text: OMB directs federal agencies to strengthen AI governance through accountable officials, inventories, and review processes for agency uses of AI.
context.title: OMB M-24-10 - Agency Governance

expected_answer: Use Map to establish the intended use, context, stakeholders, benefits, and risks. Use Measure to analyze, assess, benchmark, and monitor those risks with evidence. Govern supports the work, but Map and Measure answer the question.
query: Which NIST AI RMF functions establish the AI system context and then check whether risks are being tracked with evidence?

context.text: Govern is the cross-cutting AI RMF function. It cultivates and implements a risk management culture, policies, processes, procedures, and practices. It assigns accountability and integrates oversight across the AI lifecycle.
context.title: NIST AI RMF 1.0 - Govern Function

context.text: Measure focuses on identifying methods and metrics, evaluating risks, and tracking system performance and impacts. It is about evidence and monitoring rather than ownership.
context.title: NIST AI RMF 1.0 - Measure Function

context.text: The EU AI Act requires high-risk AI systems to meet obligations such as risk management, technical documentation, logging, transparency, human oversight, accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity.
context.title: EU AI Act - High-Risk Systems

expected_answer: The NIST AI RMF function is Govern. Govern covers accountability, policy, oversight, and risk management culture across the AI lifecycle; Measure is more about metrics and monitoring.
query: A review finds no named owner for an AI risk process. Which NIST AI RMF function most directly covers accountability, policy, and oversight?

context.text: The NIST Generative AI Profile was informed by a public working group focused on four primary considerations for generative AI: Governance, Content Provenance, Pre-deployment Testing, and Incident Disclosure. The profile maps suggested actions to the AI RMF functions.
context.title: NIST AI 600-1 - Generative AI Profile

context.text: The AI RMF organizes risk management around Govern, Map, Measure, and Manage. These functions are general risk management activities for AI systems, not the four primary generative AI considerations.
context.title: NIST AI RMF 1.0 - Core Functions

context.text: The GPT-4 system card describes model capabilities, limitations, safety evaluations, and deployment mitigations used before release.
context.title: OpenAI GPT-4 System Card

expected_answer: The four considerations are Governance, Content Provenance, Pre-deployment Testing, and Incident Disclosure. They come from the NIST Generative AI Profile, which maps suggested actions back to the AI RMF functions.
query: What four primary considerations did the NIST Generative AI Profile emphasize for generative AI risk management?

context.text: The NIST Generative AI Profile describes risks unique to or exacerbated by generative AI, including Confabulation, Data Privacy, Information Integrity, Harmful Bias or Homogenization, Dangerous or Violent Recommendations, and impacts on value chain and component integration.
context.title: NIST AI 600-1 - Overview of GAI Risks

context.text: The EU AI Act classifies AI systems by risk, including unacceptable-risk practices, high-risk systems, transparency obligations, and general-purpose AI rules.
context.title: EU AI Act - Risk-Based Framework

context.text: OMB distinguishes safety-impacting and rights-impacting AI uses and requires minimum risk management practices for agency deployments in those categories.
context.title: OMB M-24-10 - Rights-Impacting AI

expected_answer: Examples from the NIST Generative AI Profile include Confabulation, Data Privacy, and Information Integrity. Other listed risk areas include Harmful Bias or Homogenization and Dangerous or Violent Recommendations.
query: For a memo on generative AI harms, name three risk areas the NIST Generative AI Profile treats as generative-AI-specific or exacerbated by generative AI.

context.text: The EU AI Act creates a risk-based framework. It distinguishes prohibited unacceptable-risk AI practices, high-risk AI systems, transparency obligations for certain AI systems, and rules for general-purpose AI models. Unacceptable-risk AI practices are prohibited.
context.title: EU AI Act - Risk-Based Framework

context.text: High-risk AI systems are not automatically banned. They must satisfy obligations such as risk management, data governance, documentation, transparency, human oversight, accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity.
context.title: EU AI Act - High-Risk Obligations

context.text: The OECD AI Principles promote inclusive growth, human-centered values, transparency, robustness, security, safety, and accountability for trustworthy AI.
context.title: OECD AI Principles

expected_answer: The strictest category is unacceptable-risk AI practices. In the EU AI Act framework, unacceptable-risk practices are prohibited rather than merely documented or subjected to high-risk obligations.
query: What is the strictest treatment category in the EU AI Act risk framework, and what happens to practices in that category?

context.text: High-risk AI systems are subject to requirements including risk management, data governance, technical documentation, record keeping, transparency, instructions for use, human oversight, accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity.
context.title: EU AI Act - High-Risk Obligations

context.text: The strictest EU AI Act category covers unacceptable-risk practices that are prohibited. That category is separate from high-risk systems, which may be placed on the market if obligations are met.
context.title: EU AI Act - Prohibited Practices

context.text: Measure analyzes and monitors AI risks, including performance and impacts, using methods, metrics, benchmarks, and evaluation results.
context.title: NIST AI RMF 1.0 - Measure Function

expected_answer: The checklist should cover risk management, data governance, technical documentation, record keeping, transparency, human oversight, accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity for a high-risk AI system.
query: For a high-risk AI system under the EU AI Act, which compliance themes should an implementation checklist cover?

context.text: The EU AI Act adds obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models with systemic risk. Those providers must perform model evaluations, assess and mitigate systemic risks, track and report serious incidents, and ensure an adequate level of cybersecurity protection.
context.title: EU AI Act - GPAI Models With Systemic Risk

context.text: Certain AI systems have transparency duties, such as informing people when they interact with an AI system or when content is artificially generated or manipulated.
context.title: EU AI Act - Transparency Obligations

context.text: The GPT-4 system card documents safety evaluations, limitations, mitigations, and deployment considerations for a model release.

context.text: The NIST Generative AI Profile describes risk areas such as confabulation, data privacy, information integrity, and dangerous or violent recommendations.
context.title: NIST AI 600-1 - Overview of GAI Risks

expected_answer: For a GPAI model with systemic risk, the provider should expect model evaluations, systemic risk assessment and mitigation, serious-incident tracking and reporting, and adequate cybersecurity protection.
query: What extra duties should a general-purpose AI model provider expect when the EU AI Act treats the model as having systemic risk?

context.text: The EU AI Act includes transparency obligations for certain AI systems. People should be informed when they are interacting with an AI system unless the interaction is obvious from the context of use. Other transparency rules cover artificially generated or manipulated content.
context.title: EU AI Act - Transparency Obligations

context.text: High-risk AI systems must meet requirements around risk management, data governance, technical documentation, record keeping, transparency, human oversight, accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity.
context.title: EU AI Act - High-Risk Obligations

context.text: The Blueprint names notice and explanation as one of its five principles, encouraging people to know when automated systems are being used and understand outcomes that affect them.
context.title: Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights - Notice and Explanation

expected_answer: The team should inform people that they are interacting with an AI system, unless that is already obvious from the context. The same transparency family also covers generated or manipulated content.
query: If an AI service directly interacts with people, what transparency duty from the EU AI Act should the product team remember?

context.text: Executive Order 14110 directed federal action on safe, secure, and trustworthy AI. context.text: Executive Order 14110 emphasized safe, secure, and trustworthy AI. context.text: Manage is the AI RMF function for allocating risk resources, prioritizing risks, and acting on mapped and measured risks. Manage uses the context from Map and the evidence from Measure to decide and execute risk responses.
context.title: NIST AI RMF 1.0 - Manage Function

context.text: Map frames the system's intended use, context, stakeholders, benefits, and risks. It helps teams understand the risk context before deciding what to do.
context.title: NIST AI RMF 1.0 - Map Function

context.text: Measure analyzes, assesses, benchmarks, and monitors AI risks and impacts. It supplies evidence for later risk decisions.
context.title: NIST AI RMF 1.0 - Measure Function

expected_answer: Use Manage. Manage allocates risk resources, prioritizes risks, and acts on mapped and measured risks using Map context and Measure evidence.
query: After a team maps and measures AI risks, which NIST AI RMF function is about prioritizing and acting on those risks?
